1: Psalm 136 is the Great Hallel — the psalm of antiphonal praise in which a leader declares each act of YHWH and the congregation responds with the refrain "for his steadfast love endures forever (kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô)." The refrain appears 26 times, once per verse. This antiphonal structure is described in 2 Chr 7:3 (at Solomon's temple dedication) and Ezra 3:11 (at the laying of the Second Temple foundation) — the congregational response "kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô" was the standard liturgical acclamation at major moments of covenant worship. By the Mishnaic period (m. Pesaḥim 10:6-7), Ps 136 was the Great Hallel sung at the Passover meal — making Matt 26:30 ("when they had sung a hymn") possibly a reference to this psalm after the Last Supper.
2: "Give thanks to the God of gods" — the superlative title in the refrain structure: YHWH is God above all other claimants to divinity. The same formula as Deut 10:17 ("the LORD your God is God of gods and Lord of lords"). The liturgical structure of the psalm means this verse was followed by the congregation's response "kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô" — "for his steadfast love endures forever." This response grounds the theological claim (God of gods) in the relational quality that makes theology personal: ḥeseḏ, covenant loyalty. The greatest claim about God is answered by the most personal claim about his character.
3: "Give thanks to the Lord of lords" — the second superlative title, completing the triad with v. 1 (LORD) and v. 2 (God of gods). Deut 10:17 uses the same pair (God of gods / Lord of lords). Rev 17:14 and 19:16 apply this title to the risen Christ: "King of kings and Lord of lords." The psalm's liturgical repetition — each claim followed by the ḥeseḏ refrain — trains the congregation to associate divine greatness with covenant love rather than with power alone.
4: "To him who alone does great wonders" — the introduction of the creation and history sections (vv. 4-22). "Alone (lĕbaḏdô)" is the key word: no other being participates in the great wonders (niplāʾôt gĕḏōlôt). The divine works are singular — not the product of a pantheon's negotiation or a nature-cycle managed by competing deities, but the unilateral action of one God. "Great wonders" is the vocabulary of the Exodus traditions (Ps 72:18; 86:10; Job 5:9; 9:10) — establishing the theme that creation (vv. 5-9) and historical acts (vv. 10-22) are equally products of the one God's sole creative and saving power.
5: "Who made the heavens with wisdom (bĕtĕbûnāh)" — creation as an act of wisdom (tĕbûnāh — understanding, insight). Prov 3:19-20: "By wisdom the LORD laid the earth's foundations; by understanding he set the heavens in place; by his knowledge the watery depths were divided." Ps 104:24: "How many are your works, LORD! In wisdom you made them all." Creation is not arbitrary power but ordered intelligence — the cosmos reflects the divine wisdom that structured it. This theology becomes foundational for the Wisdom literature's claim that the same wisdom operating in creation is accessible to humans through Torah.
6: "Who spread out the earth above the waters (ʿal-hammayim)" — the creation cosmology: the flat earth resting on the primordial waters below (Gen 1:9-10; Ps 24:2: "he founded it on the seas and established it on the waters"). In the ANE cosmological picture, the earth was a disk floating on or resting above the cosmic underground waters (tĕhôm rabbāh — the great deep). The "spreading out (rāqaʿ)" of the earth echoes the "spreading out (rāqîaʿ)" of the heavens in Gen 1:6-8 — the same verb for the making of the visible firmament. YHWH's creative acts (vv. 5-9) mirror the first week of Gen 1.
7: "Who made the great lights" — the fourth day of creation (Gen 1:14-19): "God made two great lights." The psalm paraphrases the creation narrative, following its sequence. The "great lights (ʾôrîm gĕḏōlîm)" are distinguished only by their function (ruling day and night) rather than named as sun and moon — a deliberate avoidance of the Egyptian solar deity Ra and the Mesopotamian moon deity Sin, whose names the Hebrew words for sun (šemeš) and moon (yārēaḥ) might evoke in polemic context. The lights are demythologized: they are YHWH's creations, not deities.
8: "The sun to rule over the day" — the greater light (Gen 1:16). In Egyptian religion, the sun was Ra, the supreme deity whose daily journey across the sky was the central religious narrative. The psalm strips this mythological significance: the sun is YHWH's servant, its function is to "rule over the day" by providing light, not to be an object of worship. The antiphonal refrain that follows — "his steadfast love endures forever" — asserts that what motivates the sun's continued shining is YHWH's covenant love, not the sun deity's own nature or the demands of ritual. Creation is sustained by covenant love.
9: "The moon and stars to rule over the night" — the lesser light and the stars (Gen 1:16-18). In Mesopotamian religion, the moon (Sin/Nanna) and astral bodies (especially Venus/Ishtar) were major deities whose movements governed calendars, divination, and warfare decisions. Deut 4:19 explicitly warns Israel not to "look up to the sky and see the sun, the moon and the stars... and bow down to them." By placing moon and stars in the creation-by-divine-command sequence, Ps 136 strips all astral bodies of divine status — they are light-regulators for the night, serving the practical function of marking time. The same ḥeseḏ-refrain that follows the sun follows the moon: all creation alike serves under covenant love.
10: "Who struck down the firstborn of Egypt" — the pivot from creation (vv. 5-9) to history (vv. 10-22). The same God who made the sun and moon struck Egypt's firstborn. The continuity between cosmic creator and historical actor is the psalm's central theological claim: YHWH's ḥeseḏ operates at every scale, from cosmic structure to specific historical events. The transition is abrupt but intentional — Ps 136 is not a meditation on creation or history separately but on the single divine character (ḥeseḏ) that underlies both.
11: "And brought Israel out from among them" — Exod 12:51: "on that very day the LORD brought the Israelites out of Egypt." The language of Israel being "brought out (wayyôṣēʾ)" from among the Egyptians is the standard Exodus formula (Deut 5:6; 6:12; 13:5, 10; and dozens of occurrences). The Exodus is the paradigmatic act of divine ḥeseḏ in history — the event that defines YHWH as covenant-keeper, not merely as creator. Every subsequent use of "steadfast love" in the OT is colored by this defining act.
12: "With a mighty hand and an outstretched arm" — the standard anthropomorphic formula for the Exodus (Deut 4:34; 5:15; 7:19; 11:2; 26:8; Ps 44:3; Jer 32:21; Ezek 20:33-34). The "mighty hand (yāḏ ḥăzāqāh)" emphasizes irresistible divine power; the "outstretched arm (zĕrôaʿ nĕṭûyāh)" adds the spatial image of divine reach extending into history to rescue Israel. The formula became so standard that it functioned as a covenant-shorthand: to mention the "mighty hand and outstretched arm" was to invoke the entire Exodus tradition. The New Exodus prophets (Isa 40-55) would use the same imagery for the return from Babylon.
13: "Who divided the Red Sea (gāzar yam-sûp) in two" — the verb gāzar (to cut, divide, split) is unusually strong — not merely "parted" but cleaved in two, as a sword cuts. Ps 78:13 uses the same verb. The image of the sea being split into two halves emphasizes the completeness of the division — the people walked on dry ground between two walls of water (Exod 14:21-22). In Second Temple Judaism, the splitting of the sea was associated with the splitting of the Jordan (Josh 3-4) and later with Elijah's and Elisha's miracles with water — a typological series of covenant-defining sea-crossings.
14: "And brought Israel through the middle of it" — the crossing itself. The emphasis on "through the middle (bĕtôkô)" echoes Exod 14:22, 29 where Israel went "into the sea on dry ground, with a wall of water on their right and on their left." The geographic specificity — not around the sea, not carried over it, but through its middle — underlines the miraculous nature of the passage. In baptism theology (1 Cor 10:1-2 — "they were all baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea"), this passage through the waters became the pattern for covenant initiation through water.
15: "But swept Pharaoh and his army into the Red Sea" — Exod 14:27-28: "the water flowed back and covered the chariots and horsemen — the entire army of Pharaoh that had followed Israel into the sea. Not one of them survived." The specific mention of "Pharaoh (parʿōh) and his army" rather than merely "the Egyptians" emphasizes the defeat of the institutional power of Egyptian empire — not only soldiers but the royal military command itself was destroyed. The Song of Moses (Exod 15:4) celebrates: "Pharaoh's chariots and his army he has hurled into the sea."
16: "Who led his people through the wilderness" — the forty years of wilderness provision summarized in a single line. The guidance through the wilderness (hammôlîk ʿammô bammidbar) recalls the pillar of cloud and fire (Exod 13:21-22), the manna and quail (Exod 16), the water from the rock (Exod 17; Num 20), and the Sinai covenant (Exod 19-24). The brevity is liturgically appropriate — in the antiphonal structure, each act receives one line followed by the refrain. The wilderness period as a whole is a demonstration of sustained divine ḥeseḏ: forty years of daily provision in a humanly unlivable environment.
17: "Who struck down great kings" — the conquest narrative begins (vv. 17-22), parallel to Ps 135:10-12. "Great kings (mĕlāḵîm gĕḏōlîm)" — the same description as Deut 7:1; Josh 11:17 for the Canaanite city-states' rulers. The word "great" is relative — compared to the Egyptian empire, Canaanite city-kings were minor — but their fortified cities were formidable obstacles for a nomadic people without siege equipment. The psalm's attribution of their defeat to YHWH's direct action (rather than Israelite military tactics) follows the theological interpretation of the conquest in Deut 7:17-24: "Do not be afraid of them; remember well what the LORD your God did to Pharaoh and to all Egypt."
18: "And killed mighty kings" — the repetition of the conquest theme intensifies the point: the same divine ḥeseḏ that sustained Israel through the wilderness now clears the land before them. "Killed (wayyahărōg) mighty kings" — the verb is direct and unambiguous. The conquest involved real military violence, and the psalm does not euphemize it. The theological framing is that YHWH's covenant love toward Israel required the removal of competing powers from the land YHWH had promised. The ethical difficulties of this theology have been engaged throughout Jewish and Christian interpretation history — the psalm presents it without apology as a fact of covenant history.
19: "Sihon king of the Amorites" — the paradigmatic Transjordan defeat (Num 21:21-31; Deut 2:26-37). Sihon refused Israel passage through his territory and attacked — and was decisively defeated. His capital Heshbon (modern Hesban, Jordan) was taken. The repeated pairing of Sihon and Og in liturgical texts (Ps 135:11; Neh 9:22; Deut 3:6; Josh 2:10) made them the canonical examples of YHWH's power over kings who opposed Israel's passage. The narrative of their defeat in Num 21 and Deut 2-3 functions as a paradigm: when kings resist YHWH's plan for Israel, they are defeated.
20: "And Og king of Bashan" — Og's defeat (Num 21:33-35; Deut 3:1-11). Og was the last of the Rephaim (Deut 3:11) — the legendary pre-Israelite giant-people of Canaan and Transjordan (Gen 14:5; 15:20; 2 Sam 21:16-22). The tradition of Og's extraordinary size (Deut 3:11 describes his iron bed as 9 cubits long, ~4.5m) gave him a legendary quality that amplified the significance of his defeat. If even the Rephaite king of fortified Bashan could not resist YHWH, no human power could. The two kings together (Sihon the Amorite + Og the Rephaite) represented different ancient threats — political and primordial — both overcome by the same ḥeseḏ.
21: "And gave their land as an inheritance (naḥălāh)" — as in Ps 135:12, the conquest is framed as a gift (nātan, gave) not a military achievement. The land already belonged to YHWH (Lev 25:23: "the land is mine; you are but aliens and strangers resident with me") — he gave it to Israel as naḥălāh, his own patrimony distributed to his people. The Jubilee land laws (Lev 25) were grounded in this theology: since YHWH gave the land, human ownership is always provisional and the land must periodically revert to its original distribution.
22: "An inheritance to his servant Israel" — the closing of the historical section (vv. 10-22). "His servant Israel" — ʿabdô yiśrāʾēl is the corporate servant title, applied to the nation as a whole (Isa 41:8; 44:1-2; 45:4). It is also the title for the Servant of YHWH in Isaiah's Servant Songs (Isa 42:1; 49:3: "you are my servant, Israel, in whom I will display my splendor"). The psalm's attribution of the "servant" title to Israel as a whole creates the background against which the Isaianic question of how the corporate servant Israel can perform what it has failed to do becomes the question that the individual Servant must answer.
23: "He remembered us in our low estate (bĕšiplēnû)" — the shift to first person ("us") marks the liturgical community's appropriation of the history. The "low estate" (šiplēnû) — the condition of being brought low, humiliated, stripped of status. The same word as Hannah's "humiliation" (ʿānyî, related root) in 1 Sam 1:11. The verse may have been composed in the exilic period: Israel in Babylon was literally in its lowest historical position since the Exodus. "He remembered us" — divine remembering (zākar) is always followed by divine acting (as in Exod 2:24-25). The congregation confesses that past divine remembering grounds present hope.
24: "And freed us from our enemies" — the liberation from the "low estate" of v. 23. The brevity leaves the specific enemies unspecified — making the verse applicable across multiple historical situations (Egypt, Philistia, Assyria, Babylon). The Psalter's liturgical genius is often precisely this typological openness: by not naming the specific enemy, the verse remains perpetually applicable to whatever "enemies" any generation faces. The pattern — low estate → divine remembering → liberation — is the recurring structure of covenant rescue throughout Israel's history.
25: "Who gives food (nōtēn leḥem) to every creature" — the expansion from Israel's particular history (vv. 10-24) to universal divine provision. The present tense ("gives") marks this as ongoing rather than historical. YHWH's provision of food for "every creature (lĕkol-bāśār)" — literally all flesh — echoes Ps 104:27-28 (all creatures look to YHWH for food in due season) and Gen 1:29-30 (YHWH provides food for all living things at creation). The closing before the final doxology (v. 26) places universal creation-care alongside Israel's particular covenant history — both are expressions of the same ḥeseḏ.
26: "Give thanks to the God of heaven (ʾēl haššāmayim)" — the title "God of heaven" is rare in the Psalms but common in the post-exilic period (Ezra 1:2; 5:11-12; 7:12, 21; Neh 1:4-5; 2:4, 20; Dan 2:18-19, 37, 44; Jonah 1:9). The prevalence of this title in post-exilic texts suggests Ps 136 may have its final form in that period — the period when "God of heaven" was the Persian imperial designation for the God of the Jews (Cyrus's decree in Ezra 1:2 uses the same title). The closing "kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô" — "for his steadfast love endures forever" — is the 26th and final repetition, having covered creation, Exodus, wilderness, and conquest. Everything is grounded in and returns to ḥeseḏ.
